walkthrough.title: Unfold the Master Gameboard
walkthrough.text: Let's get Shadows Over Camelot set up. First, unfold the large master gameboard and place it in the center of the table. This is the heart of Camelot. You will see the Round Table in the middle with spaces for swords, and various quest locations around the edges. There are also twelve spaces around the outside of Camelot for siege engines. Make sure everyone can reach the board comfortably, because you will all be moving around it throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Place the Quest Boards
walkthrough.text: Now find the three double-sided quest boards. These are separate boards that go around the master gameboard. Set up the Holy Grail quest board, the Excalibur quest board, and the Lancelot and Dragon quest board. Each one has two sides showing different phases or states of the quest. Place them all with their starting side face up near the corresponding locations on the main board. These quests will be where your knights go on their adventures.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Swords
walkthrough.text: Find the sixteen sword tokens. Each sword is double-sided, with a white side and a black side. These are the key to winning or losing the game. Set them all aside in a supply pile near the Round Table area of the board. As quests are won or lost throughout the game, swords will be placed on the Round Table. White side up for victories, black side up for defeats. The game ends when the twelfth sword is placed.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Miniatures
walkthrough.text: Now let's organize all the miniatures. Place the twelve siege engine miniatures in a supply near the board. These will gradually surround Camelot as the game progresses. Set the three relic miniatures aside. These represent Excalibur, the Holy Grail, and Lancelot's Armor, which can be won through quests. Place the four Saxon warriors and four Pict warriors near their respective quest areas. Finally, set the seven knight miniatures near the board. Each player will claim one during the next step.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Coats of Arms
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the seven Coat of Arms cards face down and deal one randomly to each player. Each Coat of Arms corresponds to a specific Knight of the Round Table with a unique special power. Whatever knight you receive is the character you will play for the entire game. Take the matching knight miniature and place it on the Round Table space in the center of the board. All knights begin the game gathered at the Round Table.

walkthrough.title: Set Life Dice
walkthrough.text: Each player receives one six-sided die to track their life points. Set your life die to four. That is your starting health. Your life can range from zero to six during the game. If you ever reach zero, your knight dies. Keep your life die visible next to your Coat of Arms so everyone can see how healthy each knight is. Life management is a crucial part of the game, so keep an eye on this throughout.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Black Deck
walkthrough.text: Find all seventy-six Black cards. These are the forces of evil. Shuffle them thoroughly into a single face-down deck and place it on the designated spot on the board. Black cards drive the Progression of Evil every turn. Some are Standard Black cards that get placed on quests, and some are Special Black cards with nasty immediate effects. You will be drawing from this deck a lot, and it is never good news.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the White Deck
walkthrough.text: Now find all eighty-four White cards. These are the forces of good and your primary tools for completing quests. Shuffle them into a face-down deck and place it on its designated spot on the board. White cards include Fight cards with values for combat, Grail cards for the Holy Grail quest, and Special White cards like Merlin that provide powerful one-time effects. You will be drawing and playing these throughout the game to push back against the darkness.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Hands
walkthrough.text: Deal five White cards and one Merlin card to each player. The Merlin cards are the special ones showing the wizard. So each player starts with a hand of six cards total. Keep your hand secret from other players. This is important because the communication rules in this game are strict. You can discuss general strategy, but you may never reveal the exact names or values of cards in your hand.

walkthrough.title: Deal Loyalty Cards
walkthrough.text: This is the part that makes Shadows Over Camelot truly special. Take enough Loyalty cards for your player count. You always include exactly one Traitor card and fill the rest with Loyal Knight cards. With fewer players, there is a variant to play without a traitor, but the standard game always includes one. Shuffle these Loyalty cards and deal one face down to each player. Look at your card secretly. If you are Loyal, you are working to save Camelot. If you are the Traitor, you are secretly trying to destroy it from within. Never reveal your Loyalty card unless you are accused or choose to reveal as the Traitor.

walkthrough.title: Initial Card Sharing
walkthrough.text: Before the game begins, there is one more step. Each player selects one White card from their hand and places it face up on the table. Once everyone has revealed a card, gather all the revealed cards, shuffle them, and redistribute them evenly among all players. This gives everyone a chance to exchange a card they might not need for something potentially more useful. After redistribution, players add the received card to their hand. This is the only time you will openly share card information.

walkthrough.title: Choose the First Player
walkthrough.text: The player who received King Arthur goes first. If King Arthur is not in the game, the group can decide who starts. Play proceeds clockwise from the first player. Once everyone has their cards, their Loyalty is secret, and all knights are at the Round Table, Camelot is ready. The forces of darkness are gathering. Time to defend the realm.

Place each near its corresponding location on the master board, starting side up

Play proceeds clockwise
All knights start at the Round Table

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview
walkthrough.text: Every turn in Shadows Over Camelot has exactly two phases, and you must do both in order. First comes the Progression of Evil, where bad things happen to Camelot. Then comes your Heroic Action, where you fight back. You cannot skip either phase. Evil always advances before you get to act. This is the heartbeat of the game and it creates constant pressure. Let's break down each phase.

walkthrough.title: Phase 1: Progression of Evil
walkthrough.text: At the start of your turn, you must choose one of three bad options. Option A: draw a Black card from the deck. Option B: place a siege engine around Camelot. Option C: lose one life point. You must pick one, and none of them are good. This is what makes the game so tense. Evil advances every single turn no matter what. Choosing which evil to suffer is often the most important decision of your turn.

walkthrough.title: Black Card Types
walkthrough.text: When you draw a Black card, it can be one of two types. Standard Black cards show a quest name and get placed face up on that quest's area, making that quest harder to win. Special Black cards have an immediate nasty effect that happens right away. These might add siege engines, force discards, or cause other chaos. Here is a critical rule: if you draw a Special Black card, you can counter it by discarding three Merlin cards from your hand. The card is cancelled and nothing happens. But three Merlins is a steep price, so choose wisely.

walkthrough.title: Phase 2: Heroic Action Overview
walkthrough.text: After suffering the Progression of Evil, you get to take one Heroic Action. You must choose exactly one from five options. A: move to a new quest location. B: perform a quest-specific action at your current location. C: play a Special White card from your hand. D: heal yourself by discarding three identical cards to gain one life point. E: accuse another knight of being the Traitor, but only when conditions are met. Let's go through the important ones in detail.

walkthrough.title: Moving Between Quests
walkthrough.text: To move, you pick up your knight miniature and place it at any quest location on the board. You can move from the Round Table to any quest, or from one quest to another. Moving is your entire Heroic Action for the turn, so you do not get to do anything else that turn besides move. Planning your movement is important because some quests are solo quests. The Black Knight and Lancelot quests can only have one knight working on them at a time. Other quests like the Holy Grail and the Dragon can have multiple knights collaborating.

walkthrough.title: Combat Quests Explained
walkthrough.text: Several quests are combat-based, meaning you play Fight cards from your hand to build up card combinations. The Black Knight requires you to assemble two pairs of Fight cards. The Lancelot quest needs a full house, which is three of a kind plus a pair. The Dragon quest needs three separate three-of-a-kind sets among all knights present. You play Fight cards face down on your turn as your quest action. When the required combination is complete, the quest is won and a white sword is placed. But if too many Black cards accumulate on the quest before you finish, the quest is lost and a black sword goes on the Round Table instead.

walkthrough.title: The Excalibur Quest
walkthrough.text: The Excalibur quest works differently from combat. There is a track with Excalibur in the middle. On your turn, as your quest action, you can discard any one White card from your hand to move Excalibur one space toward the knights' side. However, certain Black cards move Excalibur toward the evil side. If Excalibur reaches the knights' end, the quest is won and the knight who pushed it there claims the Excalibur relic. If it reaches the evil end, the quest is lost. The Excalibur relic lets its holder add one to their combat card values, which is powerful.

walkthrough.title: The Holy Grail Quest
walkthrough.text: The Holy Grail quest requires knights to play Grail cards onto specific numbered spaces on the quest board. As your quest action, you play one Grail card from your hand face down on the next available Grail space. Black cards placed here cover Grail spaces and block progress. If all seven Grail spaces are filled with Grail cards before the quest is overwhelmed by Black cards, the knights win the Grail. The Holy Grail relic is arguably the most valuable in the game because it can save a knight from death once. When a knight would die, the Grail restores them to full health instead, but the relic is then lost.

walkthrough.title: Saxon and Pict Wars
walkthrough.text: The Saxon Wars and Pict Wars work similarly to each other. Enemy warriors accumulate on these quest boards through Black card draws. Knights present at these quests play Fight cards with values one through five in ascending sequence. You must build a straight, playing cards in order. If four enemy warriors are placed on either quest before the knights complete the sequence, that quest is lost. When won, one white sword is placed and the enemy warriors are removed. These quests can trigger repeatedly throughout the game, so they require ongoing attention.

walkthrough.title: Fighting Siege Engines
walkthrough.text: When your knight is at the Round Table in Camelot, you have special options. You can draw two White cards from the deck as your Heroic Action, which is a great way to stock up. Or you can fight a siege engine. To fight a siege engine, play Fight cards from your hand totaling a certain value, then roll the special eight-sided die. If your total plus the die result meets the required threshold, the siege engine is removed. Managing siege engines is critical because if twelve siege engines ever surround Camelot, the game is immediately lost.

walkthrough.title: Healing and Sacrifice
walkthrough.text: To heal, discard three cards of the same type from your hand, such as three Fight cards of the same value, and gain one life point. This is expensive but necessary when you are close to death. There is also an important mechanic called Sacrifice. Once per turn, you may choose to lose one life point to take a second Heroic Action. The second action must be different from your first. This is incredibly useful in emergencies but risky if your health is low. Dead knights lose all their cards and relics, which can be devastating for the team.

walkthrough.title: Accusation Rules
walkthrough.text: Any knight can use their Heroic Action to formally accuse another knight of being the Traitor. However, accusations can only happen once there are six or more siege engines around Camelot or six or more swords on the Round Table. When accused, the targeted knight must reveal their Loyalty card. If they are the Traitor, a white sword is added to the Round Table and the Traitor is unmasked for the rest of the game. If they are Loyal, a white sword on the Round Table is flipped to its black side. Wrong accusations hurt badly, so be certain before you accuse.

walkthrough.title: Communication Rules
walkthrough.text: Communication is key to cooperation, but there are strict limits. You may freely discuss general strategy, such as saying you could help with the Grail quest or warning that the siege engines are getting dangerous. However, you may never reveal the exact names or values of cards in your hand. You cannot say you have a Fight Five or that you are holding two Merlin cards. This restriction is what gives the Traitor room to operate. They can subtly mislead without anyone being able to verify. It also means loyal knights must build trust through their actions, not their words.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Victory Conditions
walkthrough.text: The game can end in several ways. The knights lose immediately if twelve siege engines surround Camelot, if all Loyal knights are dead, or if there are seven or more black swords when checked. Otherwise, the game continues until the twelfth sword is placed on the Round Table. At that point, count the swords. If there are more white swords than black, the knights win. If it is tied or there are more black swords, evil wins. But there is a twist: if the Traitor was never caught, two white swords are flipped to their black side before the final count. All ties favor evil. So catching the Traitor matters enormously for the endgame math.

summary.title: Turn Overview
bullets: Every turn has 2 mandatory phases in order
Phase 1: Progression of Evil (bad things happen)
Phase 2: Heroic Action (you fight back)

Cannot skip — evil always advances every turn

bullets: Standard: placed on a quest, making it harder
Special: immediate bad effect
Counter Special Black cards by discarding 3 Merlin cards

summary.title: Phase 2: Heroic Action Overview
bullets: Must choose one: (A) Move to quest, (B) Quest action, (C) Play Special White card, (D) Heal, (E) Accuse
Moving uses your entire action — no quest action that turn

summary.title: Moving Between Quests
bullets: Move knight miniature to any quest location
Some quests are solo (Black Knight, Lancelot) — one knight only
Others allow multiple knights (Holy Grail, Dragon)

bullets: Black Knight: 2 pairs of Fight cards
Lancelot: full house (3 of a kind + pair)
Dragon: 3 three-of-a-kinds (shared among knights present)
Fight cards played face down; quest lost if too many Black cards accumulate

Black cards push Excalibur toward evil side
Win = Excalibur relic (+1 to combat values)

Black cards block spaces

bullets: Available when 6+ siege engines or 6+ swords on table
Correct accusation: white sword added, Traitor unmasked
Wrong accusation: 1 white sword flips to black

bullets: Discuss general strategy freely (e.g., 'I can help at the Grail')
NEVER reveal exact card names or values

summary.title: Game End and Victory Conditions
bullets: Instant loss: 12 siege engines, all Loyal knights dead, or 7+ black swords
Normal end: 12th sword placed — majority white swords = knights win
Undetected Traitor flips 2 white swords to black before final count
All ties favor evil

walkthrough.title: Meet Your Knights
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through two full rounds with five players to see how Shadows Over Camelot works in practice. Our five knights are King Arthur, Sir Gawain, Sir Galahad, Sir Percival, and Sir Kay. Everyone has received their Loyalty card secretly. One of these five might be the Traitor, but nobody knows who. All knights start at the Round Table with their life dice set to four and a hand of six cards each. The quests are waiting, evil is stirring, and Camelot needs its champions.

walkthrough.title: King Arthur's Progression of Evil
walkthrough.text: King Arthur goes first. Remember, every turn begins with the Progression of Evil. Arthur must choose one of three bad options: draw a Black card, place a siege engine, or lose a life point. Arthur decides to draw a Black card. He flips the top card from the Black deck. It is a Standard Black card targeting the Saxon Wars. The card is placed face up on the Saxon quest area. One step closer to the Saxons overwhelming the kingdom. Not great, but at least it was not a Special Black card.

walkthrough.title: King Arthur's Heroic Action
walkthrough.text: Now Arthur takes his Heroic Action. Since he is at the Round Table, he has a special option: draw two White cards. This is one of the best early-game moves because stocking up on cards gives you more flexibility later. Arthur draws two cards from the White deck and adds them to his hand. He now has eight cards. He tells the group he has some useful cards for combat quests but does not specify which ones or their values. That would be against the communication rules.

walkthrough.title: Sir Gawain's Turn
walkthrough.text: Sir Gawain is next. For his Progression of Evil, he also draws a Black card. This one is a Standard Black card for the Holy Grail quest. It gets placed on the Grail board, covering one of the Grail spaces. That is concerning because the Grail is one of the most important quests in the game. For his Heroic Action, Gawain decides to move to the Holy Grail quest. He picks up his miniature and places it on the Grail quest board. Moving is his whole action this turn. He will start working on the Grail next turn. Remember, Gawain's special power lets him draw three White cards when he is at the Round Table, but he has already left.

walkthrough.title: Sir Galahad's Turn
walkthrough.text: Sir Galahad draws a Black card for his Progression of Evil. This time it is a Special Black card. The table groans. Special Black cards have immediate effects. This one forces every knight to discard one card. Galahad could cancel it by discarding three Merlin cards, but spending three Merlins this early would be wasteful. Everyone discards one card from their hand. For his Heroic Action, Galahad plays a Special White card. His knight power lets him play one Special White card for free once per game. He plays a Fate card, which lets him look at the top three Black cards and rearrange them. He puts the least dangerous one on top. A smart defensive play.

walkthrough.title: Sir Percival's Turn
walkthrough.text: Sir Percival's turn. For his Progression of Evil, Percival chooses to place a siege engine instead of drawing a Black card. He puts one siege engine miniature on one of the twelve spaces around Camelot. That is now one siege engine in play. Why would he choose this instead of drawing a card? Sometimes you know the Black deck is about to hit a quest that is already in trouble, and a single siege engine feels less dangerous at the moment. For his Heroic Action, Percival uses his special power to peek at the top Black card in the deck, giving the team a heads-up about what is coming. Then he moves to the Excalibur quest.

walkthrough.title: Sir Kay's Turn
walkthrough.text: Sir Kay rounds out the first round. He draws a Black card for his Progression of Evil. It is a Standard Black card for the Excalibur quest, pushing Excalibur one space toward the evil side. Good thing Percival just moved there. For his Heroic Action, Kay also moves, heading to the Saxon Wars quest. He has several Fight cards in his hand and wants to start building the combat sequence before the Saxons overwhelm the kingdom. The first round is complete. One siege engine surrounds Camelot, several quests have Black cards on them, and the knights are spreading out to address threats.

walkthrough.title: Round 2: Arthur Draws Again
walkthrough.text: Round two begins with King Arthur. For his Progression of Evil, he draws another Black card. It is another Saxon Wars card. That is now two enemy warriors building up on the Saxon quest, and Kay has only just arrived. The pressure is real. For his Heroic Action, Arthur is still at the Round Table, so he draws two more White cards. His hand is getting large. He announces to the group that he is thinking about heading to a quest next turn and asks where help is needed most. Gawain says the Grail could use support. Kay mentions the Saxons are building up fast.

walkthrough.title: Gawain Works the Grail
walkthrough.text: Sir Gawain draws a Black card for his Progression of Evil. It targets the Lancelot quest, which nobody is working on yet. For his Heroic Action, Gawain is at the Holy Grail quest, so he performs a quest action. He plays a Grail card from his hand face down onto the next available Grail space on the quest board. One space covered. The Grail quest requires filling all seven spaces with Grail cards, and one is already blocked by a Black card. Gawain is making progress, but it is going to be a long road.

walkthrough.title: A Common Mistake: Revealing Card Values
walkthrough.text: Let's pause for an important teaching moment. As Gawain plays his Grail card, Galahad exclaims: 'I have a Fight Five and two Grail cards, so I can definitely come help at the Grail.' Stop right there. This is against the rules. You are never allowed to reveal exact card names or values. Galahad should have said something like: 'I have some cards that could help at the Grail' or 'I have resources for the Grail quest.' The distinction matters because the Traitor could use exact information to counter the team's plans. Keep it vague. Always.

walkthrough.title: Galahad Corrects Course
walkthrough.text: Galahad, properly chastened, takes his turn. For his Progression of Evil, he draws a Black card. It is a Special Black card — Vivien — which forces one knight to place a random card from their hand on the bottom of the White deck. Galahad could counter with three Merlins but decides against it. He loses a card at random. For his Heroic Action, Galahad moves to the Holy Grail quest to help Gawain. Two knights on the Grail quest means they can play Grail cards twice as fast. Now Galahad will be able to contribute Grail cards on future turns.

walkthrough.title: Percival Pushes Excalibur
walkthrough.text: Sir Percival is at the Excalibur quest. For his Progression of Evil, he chooses to lose one life point rather than draw a Black card or place a siege engine. His life drops from four to three. Sometimes this is the right call when the Black deck and siege engines both feel too risky. For his Heroic Action, he discards a White card from his hand to push Excalibur one space toward the knights' side. This cancels out the push toward evil from last round. Excalibur is back to neutral. It is a tug of war, and Percival is holding the line.

walkthrough.title: Kay Fights the Saxons
walkthrough.text: Sir Kay is at the Saxon Wars quest. He draws a Black card for his Progression of Evil. It is another Saxon card, adding a third warrior to the quest. If a fourth Saxon is placed, the quest is lost. The urgency is real. For his Heroic Action, Kay plays a Fight card with value one onto the quest. This starts the ascending sequence needed to win. Knights must play Fight cards valued one through five in order. Kay's special power lets him draw an extra Fight card after any combat action, so he also draws a card from the White deck. A nice bonus that helps him keep fighting.

walkthrough.title: A Common Mistake: Forgetting Progression of Evil
walkthrough.text: Let's catch another common mistake. Imagine it is the start of round three and Arthur is excited about a plan he discussed with the group. He immediately says he wants to move to the Holy Grail quest as his Heroic Action. But wait. He has not done his Progression of Evil yet. Every turn, without exception, starts with the Progression of Evil. You always suffer evil first, then act. It is easy to forget in the excitement, but your fellow knights should remind you. The game relies on this structure to maintain tension. Evil first, heroism second. Always.

walkthrough.title: Traitor Suspicion Builds
walkthrough.text: As the game progresses, pay attention to patterns. Has one knight consistently chosen to place siege engines during their Progression of Evil when the board was not that threatening? Has someone been playing low-value Fight cards on important combat quests? Has a knight been moving to quests but never seeming to make progress? These are potential signs of a Traitor. In our example, Percival noticed that one knight has been choosing siege engines two turns in a row when the Black deck seemed safe. That is not proof of treachery, but it is worth filing away. Trust is built through actions in this game.

walkthrough.title: The Sacrifice Mechanic
walkthrough.text: Let's demonstrate the Sacrifice mechanic. It is Kay's turn again. The Saxons have three warriors on the quest, and if a fourth is placed, the quest is lost. Kay does his Progression of Evil by losing a life point, dropping to three. Then for his Heroic Action, he plays a Fight card valued two on the Saxon quest, continuing the sequence. But he realizes this quest is in dire straits. He uses Sacrifice: he loses one more life point, dropping to two, to take a second Heroic Action. This second action must be different from the first, so he plays a Fight card valued three. Two Fight cards in one turn. The sequence is now at one, two, three. Two more values to go.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Card Play
walkthrough.text: An important detail about card play: all cards played on quests are placed face down. This means other players cannot verify what values you actually played. A Loyal knight plays their best cards to help. A Traitor might play a low-value card and claim it was higher, or play the wrong type of card entirely. Since nobody can check until the quest resolves, the Traitor has room to sabotage without being obvious. This is why the face-down rule is so important to the game's tension. You are trusting your fellow knights every single turn.

walkthrough.title: Relic Powers in Action
walkthrough.text: Let's say the knights successfully complete the Excalibur quest. Percival, who was working on it, claims the Excalibur relic. A white sword is placed on the Round Table. Now Percival's combat cards are all worth one extra point whenever he plays them on combat quests. That bonus applies to every Fight card he plays for the rest of the game. Relics are powerful rewards that make future quests easier. The Holy Grail saves a knight from death. Lancelot's Armor lets you draw extra cards. Winning these quests early gives the team a significant advantage.

walkthrough.title: Siege Engine Crisis
walkthrough.text: Let's fast-forward a bit. It is now later in the game and five siege engines surround Camelot. Several Black cards and Progression of Evil choices have added them over time. Arthur is at the Round Table and decides this is the moment to fight back. For his Heroic Action, he plays Fight cards totaling value four and rolls the special eight-sided die. He rolls a three, bringing his total to seven. That meets the threshold, and one siege engine is removed. Four remain. The group breathes a little easier, but there is a long way to go. Twelve siege engines means instant defeat.

walkthrough.title: The Accusation
walkthrough.text: It is getting late in the game. Six swords are on the Round Table, which means accusations are now legal. Percival has been watching closely. One knight has repeatedly made suspicious Progression of Evil choices, played seemingly weak cards on critical quests, and moved to quests without making much progress. Percival uses his Heroic Action to formally accuse that knight. The accused knight must reveal their Loyalty card. If Percival is right, a white sword is added and the Traitor plays openly for the rest of the game with reduced powers. If wrong, a white sword flips to black. It is the highest-stakes moment in the game.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Sword Math
walkthrough.text: The game reaches its climax as the twelfth sword is about to be placed. Let's count: seven white swords and four black swords are on the table. The Dragon quest just concluded successfully, adding the twelfth sword as a white sword. That is eight white to four black. But the Traitor was never caught. Two white swords must flip to black, making the final count six white and six black. Remember, all ties favor evil. The knights lose by the narrowest possible margin. If they had caught the Traitor, it would have been eight to four, a clear victory. The Traitor mechanic changes everything about the endgame.

walkthrough.title: Learning from Defeat
walkthrough.text: That heartbreaking loss illustrates several important lessons. First, catching the Traitor is worth two swords in the final count, which is enormous. Second, the Progression of Evil choices your fellow knights make are the biggest tells. Watch those closely. Third, spreading too thin across quests means you complete them slowly, giving evil more time to accumulate Black cards. Focus your efforts. Fourth, life point management matters. Dead knights lose their cards and relics, and if all Loyal knights die, the game is lost immediately.

walkthrough.title: Key Turn Sequence Recap
walkthrough.text: Let's lock in the turn sequence one more time because it is the foundation of everything. Step one: Progression of Evil. Choose to draw a Black card, place a siege engine, or lose one life. You must pick one. Step two: Heroic Action. Choose to move, perform a quest action, play a Special White card, heal, or accuse. You must pick one. Optional: Sacrifice one life point to take a second, different Heroic Action. That is it. Two phases, both mandatory, in that order. Every turn, every knight, until the game ends.

walkthrough.title: Playing as the Traitor
walkthrough.text: If you drew the Traitor Loyalty card, your goal is the opposite of everyone else. You want Camelot to fall. Your tools are subtle. Choose Progression of Evil options that hurt the team the most. Play low-value Fight cards on combat quests and claim they are high. Steer group discussion toward abandoning winnable quests. Move to quests to look helpful without actually contributing. The key is to not be obvious. If you are caught, a white sword is added and you lose most of your power. Stay hidden, sabotage quietly, and watch the kingdom crumble.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Defend Camelot
walkthrough.text:
You now know how to set up the game, take turns, work on quests, manage the forces of evil, and watch for the Traitor among you. Shadows Over Camelot is a game of trust, teamwork, and tough choices. Every turn forces you to balance the immediate crisis against the long-term war. Will you save the Grail or hold back the Saxons? Will you trust your fellow knight or accuse them?

Check out the General Tips section for strategic advice and the Advanced Strategies section for deeper tactics. Now gather your knights, draw your swords, and defend Camelot. The realm depends on you.

summary.title: Meet Your Knights
bullets: 5 players: King Arthur, Sir Gawain, Sir Galahad, Sir Percival, Sir Kay
One might be the Traitor — nobody knows who
All start at Round Table, life at 4, hand of 6 cards

bullets: Arthur draws a Black card — Standard card targeting Saxon Wars
Card placed on Saxon quest area
3 options: draw Black card, place siege engine, or lose 1 life

bullets: At Round Table: draws 2 White cards (special Round Table option)
Hand grows to 8 cards
Tells group he has useful combat cards (without revealing specifics)

summary.title: Sir Gawain's Turn

Moves to Holy Grail quest as Heroic Action
Moving uses entire Heroic Action — cannot do quest work this turn

summary.title: Sir Galahad's Turn

Could counter with 3 Merlins but too costly this early
Uses special power to play Fate card for free, rearranges top Black cards

summary.title: Sir Percival's Turn
bullets: Places siege engine instead of drawing Black card (strategic choice)
Uses special power to peek at top Black card
Moves to Excalibur quest

summary.title: Sir Kay's Turn
bullets: Draws Black card pushing Excalibur toward evil
Moves to Saxon Wars quest with Fight cards ready
Round 1 complete: 1 siege engine, Black cards on multiple quests

summary.title: Round 2: Arthur Draws Again
bullets: Another Saxon Wars Black card — pressure building on that quest
Draws 2 more White cards at Round Table
Group discusses where Arthur should move next turn

summary.title: Gawain Works the Grail
bullets: Black card targets unattended Lancelot quest
Plays Grail card on quest board — 1 of 7 spaces filled
Grail quest needs all 7 spaces covered with Grail cards

summary.title: A Common Mistake: Revealing Card Values
bullets: WRONG: 'I have a Fight 5 and two Grail cards'
RIGHT: 'I have some cards that could help at the Grail'
Never reveal exact card names or values — Traitor exploits this

summary.title: Galahad Corrects Course

More knights on a quest = faster progress

summary.title: Percival Pushes Excalibur

Discards White card to push Excalibur toward knights

summary.title: Kay Fights the Saxons
bullets: 3rd Saxon warrior placed — 1 more and quest is lost
Plays Fight 1 to start the ascending sequence

summary.title: A Common Mistake: Forgetting Progression of Evil
bullets: WRONG: jumping straight to Heroic Action
Every turn starts with Progression of Evil — no exceptions
Fellow knights should remind you if you forget

summary.title: Traitor Suspicion Builds
bullets: Watch for patterns: repeated siege engine placements, weak fight cards
Note knights who move to quests but never seem to progress
Trust is built through actions, not words

summary.title: The Sacrifice Mechanic
bullets: Lose 1 life to take a 2nd Heroic Action (must be different from 1st)
Kay uses it to play 2 Fight cards in one turn on Saxon quest
Powerful but risky if health is low

summary.title: Understanding Card Play
bullets: All quest cards played face down — no verification
Traitor can play wrong cards or low values without detection
Trust required on every single card play

summary.title: Relic Powers in Action
bullets: Excalibur: +1 to all combat card values
Holy Grail: saves a knight from death once
Lancelot's Armor: draw extra cards
Winning relic quests early gives significant advantage

summary.title: Siege Engine Crisis
bullets: 5 siege engines surrounding Camelot — getting dangerous
Arthur fights: plays Fight cards + rolls 8-sided die
Meets threshold, removes 1 siege engine
12 siege engines = instant game loss

summary.title: The Accusation
bullets: Legal when 6+ swords or 6+ siege engines
Accused knight reveals Loyalty card
Correct: white sword added, Traitor unmasked
Wrong: white sword flips to black — high stakes

summary.title: Endgame Sword Math
bullets: 8 white, 4 black after 12th sword placed
Traitor uncaught: 2 white flip to black = 6 white, 6 black
Ties favor evil — knights lose
Catching Traitor would have meant 8-4 victory

summary.title: Learning from Defeat
bullets: Catching Traitor = 2 sword swing in final count
Watch Progression of Evil choices for biggest tells
Focus efforts — don't spread too thin across quests
Dead knights lose cards and relics; all Loyal dead = instant loss

summary.title: Key Turn Sequence Recap
bullets: Phase 1: Progression of Evil (mandatory — pick 1 of 3 bad options)
Phase 2: Heroic Action (mandatory — pick 1 of 5 options)
Optional: Sacrifice 1 life for a 2nd different Heroic Action

summary.title: Playing as the Traitor
bullets: Goal: make Camelot fall without being caught
Play low-value cards, steer discussion poorly, look busy without helping
Stay hidden — being caught adds a white sword

summary.title: Ready to Defend Camelot
bullets: Turn structure: Evil first, then Heroic Action
Trust and communication are your greatest weapons
Check General Tips and Advanced Strategies for deeper play

walkthrough.title: Always Do Progression of Evil First
walkthrough.text: This sounds obvious, but in the heat of the moment, players frequently jump straight to their Heroic Action. Build a habit of always announcing your Progression of Evil choice out loud before doing anything else. Say 'I am drawing a Black card' or 'I am placing a siege engine' every single turn. This keeps the game structured, prevents accidental cheating, and also gives the group information about what evil you chose to inflict. It is a simple habit that prevents the most common rule mistake.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Quests About to Be Lost
walkthrough.text: Not all quests are equally urgent at any given moment. Always scan the board for quests that are close to being lost. A quest with three Saxon warriors is one card away from failure. The Excalibur track near the evil side needs immediate attention. A nearly full Grail quest should be pushed to completion. When a quest fails, a black sword goes on the table, and you cannot undo that. React to emergencies first, then work on long-term goals. Triage is more important than ambition.

walkthrough.title: Communicate Without Revealing Specifics
walkthrough.text: Good communication wins games, but the rules restrict what you can share. Instead of saying exact card values, use language like: 'I am well-equipped for combat,' 'I have resources for the Grail,' or 'I could use some time to build my hand.' You can say where you plan to go and what quest you want to work on. You can discuss which quests need help. Just avoid specific card names, numbers, or counts. Think of it like talking to your teammates in a noisy room. You can convey intent without precise details.

walkthrough.title: Don't Spread Too Thin
walkthrough.text: With seven possible quests and only five to seven knights, the temptation is to cover every quest. Resist it. A knight alone on a quest progresses slowly and is vulnerable to Black cards piling up faster than they can play White cards. Instead, focus two or three knights on the most critical quests and accept that some quests will be lost. A lost quest is one black sword. Twelve siege engines or all knights dead is total defeat. Winning three quests quickly is better than slowly losing five.

walkthrough.title: Keep Your Life Above One
walkthrough.text: Life points are precious. When you are at one life, you cannot use Sacrifice for a second action, and one bad Progression of Evil choice or Special Black card could kill you. Dead knights discard all their cards and lose their relics, which devastates the team. Try to stay at two or above. If you drop to one, seriously consider healing on your next turn. Three identical cards is a steep price for one life point, but staying alive is worth it. The Holy Grail relic can save you from death once, but do not count on having it.

walkthrough.title: Watch for Traitor Signs
walkthrough.text: The Traitor has subtle tools for sabotage, but patterns emerge over time. Watch for these signs: a knight who consistently places siege engines when the Black deck seems manageable, a knight who always moves to quests but rarely seems to make progress, a knight who volunteers for solo quests where nobody can verify their card play, or a knight who pushes the group toward abandoning winnable quests. No single action is proof, but a pattern of unhelpful choices should raise suspicion.

walkthrough.title: Manage Siege Engines Proactively
walkthrough.text: Siege engines are the sneaky game-ender. They accumulate one at a time through Progression of Evil choices and Special Black cards, and suddenly you are at nine or ten. Always keep a mental count. When siege engines hit six or seven, someone needs to return to the Round Table and start fighting them. It costs Fight cards and a die roll, so it is not guaranteed, but ignoring siege engines is how most groups lose their first game. Think of the siege engine count as a timer. It is always ticking.

walkthrough.title: Merlin Cards Are Precious
walkthrough.text: You start with one Merlin card each, and they are rare in the White deck. Merlin cards can counter Special Black cards when you discard three of them, and they have other powerful uses. Do not waste them casually. Hoarding Merlins as a group gives you insurance against the worst Special Black cards. Communicate vaguely about your Merlin supply. If the group knows roughly how many Merlins are available, you can make better decisions about when to counter versus when to absorb a bad card. Treat every Merlin like gold.

walkthrough.title: Use the Round Table Wisely
walkthrough.text: The Round Table is not just a starting location. It is where you go to restock. Drawing two White cards is one of the most efficient actions in the game. When your hand is depleted from quest work, return to the Round Table to refill before heading back out. It is also the only place you can fight siege engines. Think of the Round Table as your base. Venture out to quests, accomplish what you can, then return to rearm. Knights who never return to the Round Table eventually run out of cards and become ineffective.

summary.title: Always Do Progression of Evil First
bullets: Announce your choice out loud every turn
Prevents the most common rule mistake
Gives the group information about what evil was chosen

summary.title: Prioritize Quests About to Be Lost
bullets: Scan board for quests near failure (3 Saxons, Excalibur near evil side)
Lost quests add black swords permanently
Triage emergencies before long-term goals

summary.title: Communicate Without Revealing Specifics
bullets: Say 'I am well-equipped for combat' — not 'I have a Fight 5'
Share intent and plans, not card details
Traitor exploits exact information

summary.title: Don't Spread Too Thin
bullets: Focus 2-3 knights on critical quests rather than covering all 7
Solo knights are overwhelmed by Black card accumulation
Winning 3 quests fast > slowly losing 5

summary.title: Keep Your Life Above One
bullets: At 1 life: no Sacrifice, one bad event kills you
Dead knights lose all cards and relics
Heal (3 identical cards for 1 life) when at 1-2 health

summary.title: Watch for Traitor Signs
bullets: Consistent siege engine placement when Black deck is safe
Moving to quests without making progress
Volunteering for solo quests (unverifiable card play)
Steering group away from winnable quests

summary.title: Manage Siege Engines Proactively
bullets: Keep mental count — 12 = instant loss
At 6-7 siege engines, someone must fight them at Round Table
Think of siege count as a constant timer

summary.title: Merlin Cards Are Precious
bullets: Counter Special Black cards with 3 Merlins — expensive but powerful
Rare in the White deck — hoard them
Communicate vaguely about group Merlin supply

summary.title: Use the Round Table Wisely
bullets: Draw 2 White cards at the Round Table — very efficient
Only place to fight siege engines
Return to restock when hand is depleted

walkthrough.title: Quest Prioritization Order
walkthrough.text: Not all quests are created equal. The Holy Grail should almost always be your top priority because the relic saves a knight from death, which is game-changing. Excalibur is second because the permanent plus one to combat values accelerates every future combat quest. Saxon and Pict Wars are third because they recur and provide steady white swords. The Dragon is powerful but requires three knights and three three-of-a-kind sets, so it is a late-game push. The Black Knight and Lancelot are solo quests that can be worked on opportunistically. Prioritize quests that give relics first, then quests that give consistent white swords.

walkthrough.title: When to Accuse
walkthrough.text: Timing your accusation is critical. Too early and you might be wrong, flipping a white sword to black. Too late and the Traitor's damage is already done. The sweet spot is when you have seen enough patterns to be confident but before the endgame sword count makes the Traitor's hidden penalty devastating. If you are at ten swords with seven white and three black, catching the Traitor now keeps it at seven to three. But if you wait until twelve swords at eight to four, the Traitor's two-sword flip makes it six to six, which is a loss because ties favor evil. Accuse when you are confident and when the math still works in your favor.

walkthrough.title: The Grail Is Life Insurance
walkthrough.text: The Holy Grail relic does not just prevent one death. It changes how aggressively the entire team can play. When one knight holds the Grail, that knight can use Sacrifice more freely, take riskier Progression of Evil choices, and push harder on quests without worrying about dying. The Grail effectively gives the team an extra life, which means more total actions over the course of the game. Rush the Grail quest early with two knights, win it, and then leverage that safety net for the rest of the game. Give the Grail to the knight most likely to be in danger or the one using Sacrifice most frequently.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Traitor
walkthrough.text: Advanced Traitor detection goes beyond watching for suspicious actions. Pay attention to the gap between what a knight says and what they do. A Traitor who says 'I have good cards for this quest' and then seems to contribute nothing is suspect. Watch Progression of Evil choices relative to board state. A Loyal knight drawing a Black card when siege engines are at ten is reasonable. A knight adding a siege engine when siege engines are at ten is either foolish or treacherous. Also watch who advocates loudest for abandoning quests. The Traitor wants you to give up on quests that are still winnable.

walkthrough.title: Sacrifice Timing
walkthrough.text: Sacrifice is one of the most powerful tools in the game when used correctly. The best time to Sacrifice is when completing a quest this turn prevents a black sword that would otherwise be inevitable. Spending one life to win a quest and place a white sword is almost always worth it. The worst time to Sacrifice is when you are at two life or lower, because the life loss plus an unlucky Progression of Evil next turn could kill you. Plan your Sacrifices when you are at three or four life and the payoff is immediate. Do not Sacrifice for marginal gains.

walkthrough.title: Card Economy Management
walkthrough.text: Think of your hand as a shared resource. Every card played on a quest is a card you cannot use elsewhere. Healing costs three cards for one life, which is terrible card economy but sometimes necessary. Drawing at the Round Table gives two cards for one action, which is the best rate in the game. The key insight is that card flow matters more than individual card value. A knight who cycles between the Round Table and quests, playing cards efficiently and restocking regularly, contributes more than a knight who burns their entire hand on one quest and then spends three turns recovering. Maintain a hand of at least three to four cards at all times.

walkthrough.title: Relic Power Optimization
walkthrough.text: Each relic should go to the knight who benefits most. Excalibur belongs on the knight who will be doing the most combat, likely someone heading to the Dragon quest or fighting siege engines. The Holy Grail should go to the knight with the lowest life or the one planning to use Sacrifice aggressively. Lancelot's Armor should go to the knight who needs the most card draw. Discuss relic assignment before completing quests so the right knight is in position to claim it. Relics that sit on the wrong knight are partially wasted.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Sword Math
walkthrough.text: Always count swords and plan ahead. You need more white swords than black after the twelfth is placed, accounting for the Traitor penalty. If the Traitor is uncaught, you need at least nine white swords out of twelve to win, because two will flip to black making it seven to five. If the Traitor is caught, you need seven white swords out of twelve. That is a massive difference. Late in the game, every quest decision should be filtered through the sword math. Sometimes it is better to intentionally lose a quest you cannot win quickly, accepting the black sword, rather than tying up knights who could be winning a different quest for a white sword.

walkthrough.title: Solo Quest Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Solo quests like the Black Knight and Lancelot are double-edged swords. Only one knight can work on them, which means no one can verify the cards being played. This makes them ideal for the Traitor to sabotage and ideal for Loyal knights to prove their worth. If you suspect someone, watch how they perform on solo quests. Do they consistently lose them? Conversely, if you are Loyal and under suspicion, winning a solo quest is powerful evidence of your loyalty. Advanced players use solo quest assignments strategically to test and verify other players.

walkthrough.title: Controlling the Narrative as Traitor
walkthrough.text: For advanced Traitor play, the most powerful tool is not sabotage but misdirection. Accuse a Loyal knight early to flip a white sword to black and create chaos. Advocate passionately for a quest that is already lost to waste the team's resources. Volunteer for the Grail quest and play non-Grail cards face down. The best Traitors do not look suspicious. They look like the most helpful knight at the table while quietly ensuring that every quest takes one turn longer than it should. Death by a thousand cuts is more effective than obvious sabotage.

summary.title: Quest Prioritization Order
bullets: 1st: Holy Grail (relic saves from death)
2nd: Excalibur (relic gives permanent +1 combat)
3rd: Saxon/Pict Wars (recurring, steady white swords)
4th: Dragon (powerful but resource-heavy, late-game)
5th: Black Knight/Lancelot (solo, opportunistic)

bullets: Sweet spot: enough pattern evidence, before endgame sword count

Wrong accusation flips 1 white to black — be confident

summary.title: The Grail Is Life Insurance
bullets: Enables aggressive Sacrifice use for entire team
Rush with 2 knights early for maximum benefit
Give to the knight most likely to use Sacrifice

summary.title: Reading the Traitor
bullets: Watch gap between what a knight says and does
Analyze Progression of Evil choices relative to board state
Note who advocates for abandoning winnable quests

summary.title: Sacrifice Timing
bullets: Best: when it completes a quest and prevents a black sword
Worst: when at 2 or fewer life points
Plan Sacrifices at 3-4 life with immediate payoff

summary.title: Card Economy Management
bullets: Round Table draw (2 cards/action) is best card rate
Healing (3 cards for 1 life) is expensive — avoid when possible
Maintain 3-4 card minimum in hand at all times
Card flow > individual card value

summary.title: Relic Power Optimization
bullets: Excalibur to the most combat-active knight
Holy Grail to the lowest-life or most aggressive knight
Lancelot's Armor to the knight needing card draw
Discuss relic assignment before quest completion

summary.title: Endgame Sword Math
bullets: Traitor uncaught: need 9+ white swords (2 will flip)
Traitor caught: need 7+ white swords
Sometimes better to lose a slow quest and win a fast one

Use solo quest assignments to test suspected players

Make every quest take one turn longer than it should

entries.definition: The leader of the knights. King Arthur's special power allows him to trade one White card from his hand with any other knight once per turn. This facilitates resource sharing and coordination among the team.

When Sir Gawain is at the Round Table and chooses to draw White cards as his Heroic Action, he draws three cards instead of the usual two. This makes him an efficient card collector who should return to the Round Table regularly.
Sir Galahad may play one Special White card for free once per game without using his Heroic Action. This is a powerful one-time ability that should be saved for a critical moment.

After Sir Kay performs any combat-related quest action (playing Fight cards), he draws one additional card from the White deck. This makes him the best sustained combat knight, ideal for long quest sequences.
Sir Palamedes has the ability to place any White card (not just Grail cards) on the Holy Grail quest. This flexibility makes him extremely valuable for completing the Grail quest efficiently.
Sir Percival can peek at the top card of the Black deck at any time during his turn. This intelligence-gathering power lets the group anticipate threats and make better Progression of Evil decisions.
Sir Tristan can move from the Round Table to any quest without spending his Heroic Action. This means he can move and act on the same turn, making him the most mobile knight on the team.

A cooperative quest where knights play Grail cards onto seven numbered spaces on the quest board. Black cards block spaces. Completing the quest awards the Holy Grail relic and a white sword. Losing it places a black sword. Multiple knights can work on it simultaneously.

A tug-of-war quest with Excalibur on a track. Knights discard any White card to move Excalibur one space toward their side. Black cards push it toward evil. Reaching the knights' end wins the Excalibur relic and a white sword. Multiple knights can contribute.

A solo combat quest requiring two pairs of Fight cards to complete. Only one knight can work on this quest at a time. Winning places a white sword. Black cards accumulate and if the quest is overwhelmed, a black sword is placed instead.

A solo combat quest requiring a full house (three of a kind plus a pair) of Fight cards. Only one knight at a time. Winning awards a white sword and potentially the Lancelot's Armor relic. Losing places a black sword.

A group combat quest requiring three separate three-of-a-kind sets of Fight cards shared among all knights present. The most demanding combat quest. Winning places a white sword. Multiple knights should collaborate.

A recurring combat quest. Saxon warriors accumulate through Black cards. Knights must play Fight cards valued one through five in ascending order. If four Saxons are placed before completion, the quest is lost. Can trigger multiple times per game.

Functions identically to Saxon Wars but with Pict warriors. A separate recurring quest that requires its own ascending Fight card sequence. Both Saxon and Pict Wars can be active simultaneously, stretching knight resources.

The central starting location on the board. Knights at the Round Table can draw two White cards or fight siege engines as their Heroic Action. It is the primary card-drawing location and siege engine defense point.

Standard White cards with numerical values used in combat quests. Played face down on quest boards to build required combinations (pairs, full house, three-of-a-kind, or ascending sequences). Values range from one to five.

Standard White cards specifically used for the Holy Grail quest. Played onto numbered Grail spaces on the quest board. Cannot be used for combat but can be discarded for the Excalibur quest or healing.
Standard White cards that can be used to advance the Excalibur quest. However, any White card can be discarded to push Excalibur, so these are not exclusively required.

The most valuable Special White card. Three Merlin cards can be discarded to cancel any Special Black card's effect. Merlin cards can also be played individually for various beneficial effects. Each player starts with one. Extremely rare in the White deck.

A Special White card that lets the player look at the top several cards of the Black deck and rearrange their order. Provides valuable intelligence about upcoming threats and lets the team prepare for what is coming.

A Special White card that provides a powerful one-time combat bonus or allows a knight to take an extra action. Used as a Heroic Action to play. Save for critical moments when a quest needs to be won immediately.
A Special White card that allows all knights to draw additional cards. Benefits the entire team when played, making it one of the best group-support cards available.
A Special White card that can remove threats from the board such as siege engines or Black cards placed on quests. Valuable for emergency threat management.

A Special Black card with a powerful negative effect. When drawn, Morgan forces specific harmful actions such as forcing knights to discard cards or adding threats to the board. Can be countered by discarding three Merlin cards.

One of the most dangerous Special Black cards. Mordred's effect can cause devastating setbacks such as flipping swords, adding multiple siege engines, or other severe penalties. Counter with three Merlin cards if possible.

A Special Black card that forces a knight to place a random card from their hand on the bottom of the White deck. Targets the drawing player and weakens their hand unpredictably. Counterable with three Merlin cards.

The majority of the Black deck. Each Standard Black card shows a quest name and is placed face up on that quest's area when drawn. They make quests harder by adding obstacles, enemy progress, or pushing quest conditions toward failure.

A subset of the Black deck with immediate, often devastating effects. When drawn during Progression of Evil, their effect triggers immediately. The only way to cancel them is by discarding three Merlin cards from your hand.

Won by completing the Excalibur quest. The knight holding Excalibur adds one to the value of every Fight card they play on combat quests. This permanent bonus makes all future combat significantly easier. Lost if the holding knight dies.

Won by completing the Holy Grail quest. When the holding knight would die (life reaches zero), the Grail restores them to full health instead. The relic is then removed from the game. Arguably the most powerful relic. Lost if holder dies by other means first.

Won by completing the Lancelot quest. The knight holding the Armor gains additional card draw benefits, allowing them to build their hand more efficiently. Provides a sustained advantage for the remainder of the game. Lost if the holding knight dies.

Placed on the Round Table when quests are won. White swords represent the forces of good. At game end, the knights need a majority of white swords among the twelve on the table to win. Each won quest typically adds one white sword.

Placed on the Round Table when quests are lost or wrong accusations are made. Black swords represent evil's victories. Seven or more black swords at any time ends the game in defeat for the knights. All ties in sword count favor evil.

Miniatures placed around Camelot through Progression of Evil choices and Special Black cards. If twelve siege engines ever surround Camelot, the game is immediately lost. Knights at the Round Table can fight siege engines using Fight cards and the eight-sided die.

Dealt secretly during setup. Each player receives either a Loyal Knight card or the single Traitor card. Loyal knights work together to save Camelot. The Traitor secretly works to destroy it. Loyalty is only revealed through accusation or voluntary Traitor reveal.

A Loyalty card indicating the player is working to save Camelot. Loyal knights cooperate on quests, fight evil, and try to identify the Traitor. If all Loyal knights die, the game is immediately lost regardless of sword count.

A Loyalty card indicating the player secretly works against the knights. The Traitor sabotages quests, makes harmful Progression of Evil choices, and tries to avoid detection. If uncaught at game end, two white swords flip to black. If caught, a white sword is added to the table.

One of three Progression of Evil options. Draw the top card from the Black deck. If Standard, place it on the indicated quest. If Special, resolve its immediate effect (or counter with three Merlin cards). The most common Progression choice.

One of three Progression of Evil options. Take one siege engine from the supply and place it on an empty space around Camelot. Safer than drawing a Black card when quests are in trouble, but contributes to the twelve-engine loss condition.

One of three Progression of Evil options. Reduce your life die by one. The safest option for the board state but directly weakens your knight. Risky if already at low health. Sometimes the best choice when both cards and siege engines are dangerous.

A Heroic Action that lets you relocate your knight miniature to any quest location on the board. Uses your entire Heroic Action — you cannot perform a quest action on the same turn you move (unless you use Sacrifice for a second action).

A Heroic Action where you contribute to the quest at your current location. This means playing Fight cards at combat quests, Grail cards at the Grail, or discarding White cards at Excalibur. The specific action depends on the quest type.
A Heroic Action where you play one Special White card from your hand (such as Merlin, Fate, or Heroism). The card's effect resolves immediately. This uses your Heroic Action for the turn.

A Heroic Action where you discard three identical White cards from your hand to gain one life point (up to the maximum of six). Expensive in card resources but necessary to avoid death. Cannot heal above six.

A Heroic Action available only when there are six or more siege engines around Camelot or six or more swords on the Round Table. Point at a knight and they must reveal their Loyalty card. Correct accusation adds a white sword. Wrong accusation flips a white sword to black.

An optional mechanic allowing you to lose one life point to take a second Heroic Action on the same turn. The second action must be different from the first. Can only be used once per turn. Powerful but risky at low health.

Players may discuss general strategy, intentions, and which quests need help. Players may never reveal the exact names, types, or values of specific cards in their hand. Saying 'I can help with combat' is allowed. Saying 'I have a Fight Five' is not.

Cards dealt randomly during setup that assign each player their knight identity. Each Coat of Arms corresponds to one of the seven Knights of the Round Table and determines that player's special power for the entire game.

Tracked by a six-sided die showing values zero through six. Starting value is four. A knight dies when reaching zero (unless saved by the Holy Grail). Dead knights discard all cards and relics. Life can be lost through Progression of Evil, Sacrifice, or Special Black cards.

A special die used when fighting siege engines at the Round Table. Rolled after playing Fight cards, the die result is added to the total Fight card value. If the combined total meets the required threshold, the siege engine is removed.

A knight holding twelve or more cards cannot draw additional White cards. This soft limit encourages playing cards on quests rather than hoarding. The limit applies to drawing only — you can still receive cards through other means.

All cards played on quests are placed face down. No other player can look at them until the quest resolves. This is a core mechanic that enables Traitor deception — the Traitor can play wrong or weak cards without immediate detection.

The Black Knight and Lancelot quests can only have one knight working on them at a time. No other knight may move to a solo quest that is already occupied. Solo quests provide opportunities for individual heroism or undetectable Traitor sabotage.

The knights lose immediately if any of these occur: twelve siege engines surround Camelot, seven or more black swords are on the Round Table, or all Loyal knights are dead. These are checked continuously throughout the game.

The game normally ends when the twelfth sword is placed on the Round Table. Count white versus black swords. If the Traitor is uncaught, flip two white swords to black first. Majority white swords means the knights win. Ties favor evil.
